coredata.dc:title: Application of molecularly imprinted polymers and dual-emission carbon dots hybrid for ratiometric determination of chloramphenicol in milk
coredata.dc:creator: Jalili, Roghayeh
Khataee, Alireza
coredata.dc:description: Chloramphenicol (CLP) is a veterinary antibiotic that has been banned due to its severe side effects but it is still illegally used in animal husbandry. In this work, the fabrication of simple, fast-response and highly selective ratiometric probe for sensitive visual detection of CLP antibiotic at trace levels in both indoor and outdoor is reported. For the construction of the ratiometric fluorescence probe (mMIP@YBCDs), two kinds of different carbon dots with yellow emission (Y/CDs, 560 nm) and blue emission carbon dots (B/CDs, 440 nm) were used as target sensitive and as reference dyes respectively. Besides, molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica was used as a recognized part of the probe. Upon the addition of different concentrations of CLP, the fluorescence of Y/CDs was quenched significantly while the fluorescence intensity of B/CDs stayed constant which was accompanied by gradual fluorescence color change from yellow-to-blue. The ratiometric probe has a linear response in the range of 0.1–3 μgL−1 with a detection limit 0.035 μgL−1. The practicality of the ratiometric method was validated by the quantification of CLP in milk samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometry
Chloramphenicol
Carbon dots
Nanosensor
Point of care testing

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Nanomaterial-based technologies for determination of food toxicity, Edited by: Dr. Saura Sahu, Dr. Alireza Khataee, Dr. Amin Mousavi Khaneghah and Dr. Yasser Vasseghian

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and optical properties of mMIP@YBCDs
Analytical performance of mMIP@YBCDs ratiometric probe

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: The Ministry of Economic Affairs and Employment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is supported by a research grant (Post Doc) of the University of Tabriz ( S2883-98.7.7 ). The authors thank the Iran University of Medical Sciences for all the support provided (Project No: (Project No: 97-4-75-13659 ).

The surface morphology, structure, and size distribution of mMIP@YBCDs were investigated by SEM (Fig. 2a) and TEM (Fig. 2b). TEM image illustrated that mMIP@YBCDs has a highly monodispersed, spherical morphology with a diameter of about 50 nm. SEM image (Fig. 3a inset) provided further support for the highly spherical morphology of mMIP@YBCDs.This research is supported by a research grant (Post Doc) of the University of Tabriz (S2883-98.7.7). The authors thank the Iran University of Medical Sciences for all the support provided (Project No: (Project No: 97-4-75-13659).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of B/CDs (black curve), Y/CDs (red curve) and mMIP@YBCDs (green curve). The digital images of (right) B/CDs, (left) Y/CDs under ultraviolet light of 365 nm are shown in the inset.
(a) SEM image and (b) TEM image of mMIP@YBCDs, (c) HR-TEM image of Y/CDs, (d) HR-TEM image of B/CDs, (e) SEM image and (f) TEM image of mNIP@YBCDs

(a) The fluorescence spectra of mMIP@YBCDs in different concentrations of CLP (The concentrations of CLP were 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.8, 1.35, 1.9 and 3 μg L−1, respectively), (b) calibration regression curve of mMIP@YBCDs (black curve) and mNIP@YBCDs (red curve) (The experimental conditions were λex = 400 nm, pH = 8 and incubation times = 5 min) and (c) color changes of mMIP@YBCDs ratiometric probe in the presence of different concentrations of CLP. (d) The fluorescence spectra of mNIP@YBCDs in the presence of different concentrations of CLP. (The concentration of CLP were 0.7, 1, 1.5, 1.9, 2.55 and 2.9 μg L−1, respectively).
The selectivity of the mMIP@YBCDs and mNIP@YBCDs to other template structural analogs.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure for mMIP@YBCDs and its application in CLP sensing.
Analytical characteristics of some reported methods for the quantification of CLP.

head.ce:title: Application of molecularly imprinted polymers and dual-emission carbon dots hybrid for ratiometric determination of chloramphenicol in milk

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chloramphenicol (CLP) is a veterinary antibiotic that has been banned due to its severe side effects but it is still illegally used in animal husbandry. In this work, the fabrication of simple, fast-response and highly selective ratiometric probe for sensitive visual detection of CLP antibiotic at trace levels in both indoor and outdoor is reported. For the construction of the ratiometric fluorescence probe (mMIP@YBCDs), two kinds of different carbon dots with yellow emission (Y/CDs, 560 nm) and blue emission carbon dots (B/CDs, 440 nm) were used as target sensitive and as reference dyes respectively. Besides, molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica was used as a recognized part of the probe. Upon the addition of different concentrations of CLP, the fluorescence of Y/CDs was quenched significantly while the fluorescence intensity of B/CDs stayed constant which was accompanied by gradual fluorescence color change from yellow-to-blue. The ratiometric probe has a linear response in the range of 0.1–3 μgL−1 with a detection limit 0.035 μgL−1. The practicality of the ratiometric method was validated by the quantification of CLP in milk samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Chloramphenicol (CLP), is a broad-spectrum antibiotic that has been widely applied in veterinary and human for the treatment of gram-positive and gram-negative infections. However, many studies have revealed that the use of CLP can cause severe side effects, including a plastic anemia, leukemia, gray-syndrome and bone marrow suppression, in humans. The Joint Food and Agriculture Organization/World Health Organization (FAO/WHO) Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA) concluded that it was not appropriate to establish an acceptable daily intake (ADI) for CLP (JECFA, 2015). Most countries have banned the clinical use of CLP in food production and processing (Bacanlı and Başaran, 2019; Tajik et al., 2010). Nevertheless, due to its low cost, accessibility and significant effects on the infection control, CLP is still illegally used in animal husbandry (Du et al., 2016; Rodziewicz and Zawadzka, 2008; Zhou et al., 2019).

Among the reported methods for detection of CLP, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and LC-MS/MS techniques are the most common (Chen et al., 2020; Kikuchi et al., 2017; C. Liu et al., 2019a; Zhang et al., 2006; Zhao et al., 2020). The LC-MS/MS technique needs time and labor-intensive sample pretreatment steps and also expensive instrumentation and expertise. ELISA method is tedious and time-consuming and requires very expensive and disposable antibodies. In addition, natural antibodies suffer from poor stability and the necessity for careful storage and handling. Therefore, developing point of care, fast and sensitive methods to monitor CLP levels effectively in foodstuff is of great importance.

The molecularly imprinting polymers (MIPs) are the most promising approach for antibody mimicking. For the preparation of MIPs, functional monomers and cross-linkers self-assemble around the target molecule (template), according to the exposed functionalities. The polymerization reaction “freezes” the monomers in place. Removing the template generates binding sites in the 3D polymer network which is complementary in size, shape, and functionality to the template(Ejeian et al., 2019; Amjadi and Jalili, 2016; Ansari, 2017).

Although molecular imprinting is an old idea, its potential as molecular recognition tool in sensors has been revived recently (Özcan et al., 2020; Pelin Böke et al., 2020). There are numerous examples of MIP based sensors that use optical (Ensafi et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019), electrochemical (Kadirsoy et al., 2020) and acoustic (Tretjakov et al., 2016) transducers to generate signals in response to the template binding to the imprinting site. Among all the transduction mechanisms fluorescence has attracted significant attention because of its intrinsically superb sensitivity, simplicity and high spatial resolution that principally allow for a multitude of signaling schemes.

Mesoporous silica materials have attracted great attention as an imprinting matrix because their rigid structures are highly suitable for the formation of a delicate recognition site. Furthermore, their high pore volume and nanosized pore wall thickness guarantee the generation of recognition sites near the surface that greatly decrease mass-transport resistance and provide easier accessibility to template molecules (Kim et al., 2012).

Recently, ratiometric fluorescent methods have widely attracted attention due to their eliminated environmental interferences and visual signal output. Different from the traditional single fluorescence detection methods, the ratiometric fluorescence approach by utilizing the ratio variations in two well-resolved fluorescent intensities as the detection signal can provide a built-in correction to accurate analysis (Jalili et al., 2019). In the ratiometric fluorescence probes, the semiquantitative concentration of analyte is easier to evaluate by the naked eye that makes them candidates for point of care tests. One of the keys for the successful design of the ratiometric fluorescence probe is choosing appropriate fluorophores (Jalili et al., 2020b). The fluorophores in a ratiometric probe structure should have high quantum yields, good overlap in excitation spectra and well-resolved sharp emission spectra. Because of their high fluorescent brightness, photostability, and tunable emissive color carbon dots (CDs) can better meet the above requirements. To the best of our knowledge, very few have been employed different types of carbon dots as ratiometric probe fluorophores. Compared to traditional fluorophores (Irani-nezhad et al., 2019; Jalili and Khataee, 2019; Jalili et al., 2020a) (semiconductor quantum dots, metal nanoclusters and dyes) CDs show outstanding advantages such as biocompatibility, stable photoluminescence, excellent chemical inertness, easy synthesis and low cost(Jalili et al., 2020b; Molaei, 2020).

Herein, we report a highly selective molecularly imprinted ratiometric nanoprobe (mMIP@YBCDs) for the efficient detection of CLP antibiotic. We used blue-emitting (B/CDs) and yellow emitting (Y/CDs) carbon dots as a reference and a signal report unit of ratiometric probe respectively, and a mesoporous silica nanoparticle was used as an imprinting material. In this ratiometric probe, B/CDs (440 nm) were embedded in the core of nanoprobe and used as the reference unit because of their stable fluorescence emission while Y/CDs were embedded in the imprinted mesoporous shell and interacted with CLP resulting the strong fluorescence quenching at 560 nm (Scheme 1 ). The intensity ratio changes which could be observed with the naked eye in 5 min, enabled ratiometric CLP quantification with a detection limit of 0.035 μgL−1. Practicably, the proposed probe managed field test of CLP in milk samples, demonstrating the superiority of mMIP@YBCDs probe. It is anticipated that the point of care detection ability and rapid and accurate response of this method will extend the usefulness of this technology for health food agencies, industrial quality control, and veterinary purposes.

O-phenylenediamine (O-PD), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and urea were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (US). Chloramphenicol (CLP), thiamphenicol (THM), florfenicol (FLF), tetracycline (TCY) and gentamicin (GMC) were obtained from Dana Pharmaceutical Co (Tabriz, Iran). Citric acid, amino acids and other common solvents and salts were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Double distilled water (DDW) and dehydrated ethanol were used throughout the experimental procedures.

Fluorescence (FL) experiments were performed on a Perkin Elmer LS45 (USA) fluorimeter instrument coupled with a 1.00 cm quartz cell and under excitation of 400 nm. The UV–vis absorption data were gathered using a Shimadzu spectrophotometer (Japan). The structure and morphology of mMIP@YBCDs were investigated by a Tescan (Czech Republic) scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Philips, the Netherlands). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were taken at 200 kV by JEM-2200FS (Japan). N2 adsorption–desorption analysis (BET surface) was done at 77 K on a Quantachrom C (USA) apparatus.

B/CDs were synthesized according to a previous route with some modifications (Hai et al., 2018). A solution was prepared via dissolving of urea (0.2 g) and citric acid (0.42 g) in 50 mL water and that was then put into a stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon liner of 150 mL capacity. The autoclave was sealed and heated at 180 °C for 4 h and then naturally cooled down in room temperature. The obtained B/CDs solution was purified using 1 kDa dialysis bag to remove the excess impurities and residual reactants. The pH of the clear solution of B/CDs was adjusted at 7.0 with NaOH.

According to published methods (Bao et al., 2015), for preparation of Y/CDs, 1 g of o-PD was dissolved in 100 mL of water and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C in an electric oven for 12 h. After cooling at room temperature, the resultant mixture was centrifuged to collect the precipitate (Y/CDs). The as-obtained Y/CDs were washed with ultrapure water and stirred overnight. The product was centrifuged. Then templates (CLP and CTAB) were omitted from the resultant precipitate using water and ethanol as extracting solvent. The washing (extract) and centrifuge cycle were continued until no template was observed in the supernatant solution (the presence of templates was examined by UV–Vis absorption of supernatant solution). The as-prepared mMIP@YBCDs were re-dispersed in water and kept at 4 °C. Non-imprinted particles (mNIP@YBCDs) as control were synthesized identically, except that the template (CLP) wasn't added.

mMIP@YBCDs ratiometric probe was prepared via a simple sol-gel based method according to previous work (Amjadi and Jalili, 2017). First, 500 μL of B/CDs and 150 μL of ammonia (25%) were injected into a mixture of 15 mL of ethanol and 75 mL of deionized water and stirring was done vigorously. Then, 100 μL of TEOS dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol and added dropwise to the above mixture. After stirring for 8 h, the resultant B/CDs embedded silica nanoparticles (B/CDs@silica) were separated by centrifuge. After washing 3 times with water and ethanol, B/CDs@silica nanoparticles dispersed thoroughly in 50 ml deionized water with the aid of ultrasonic. In the next step, the mesoporous CLP imprinted layer was synthesized on the B/CDs@silica nanoparticles surface. Briefly, 2.5 mL of Y/CDs, 60 μL of APTES and 50 mg of CLP (template) were dissolved into 50 mL water and injected to the 50 ml B/CDs@silica nanoparticles suspension. After the addition of 2.0 mL of CTAB (0.2 M), 0.4 mL of NaOH (0.2 M) and 150 μL of TEOS, the mixture solution was magnetically stirred overnight. The product was centrifuged. Then templates (CLP and CTAB) were omitted from the resultant precipitate using water and ethanol as extracting solvent. The washing (extract) and centrifuge cycle were continued until no template was observed in the supernatant solution (the presence of templates in the supernatant solution was examined using UV absorption). The as-prepared mMIP@YBCDs nanoparticles were in 100 ml deionized water and kept in the refrigerator. For investigating the imprinting process efficiency, Non-imprinted particles (mNIP@YBCDs) were synthesized identically, except that the template (CLP) wasn't added.

The assay protocol was as follows: 100 μL of stock suspension of mMIP@YBCDs, 500 μL phosphate buffer solution (pH = 8, 10 mM) and a given concentration of CLP standard solution or sample were poured into a 5 mL volumetric flask. Then the mixture was diluted up to 5 mL with double distilled water and stirred for 6 min. The standard and sample solutions were transferred into a standard 1 × 1 quartz cell and the fluorescence spectra of them were obtained at excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The control experiments of mNIP@YBCDs were the same as that of mMIP@YBCDs. The proposed ratiometric probe was exerted for the quantification of CLP concentration in raw milk samples collected from the local market. Milk samples were diluted 20 -fold using water before the analysis without any special treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and optical properties of mMIP@YBCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Loading two kinds of CDs into mMIP@YBCDs ratiometric probe structure controlled simply through ordered silica coating. First, the B/CDs (emission peak centered at 440 nm) were incorporated into silica nanoparticles via simple sol-gel reaction to obtain B/CDs embedded silica nanoparticles (B/CDs@silica). The silica coating around the B/CDs hampers the direct contact of B/CDs with the solution and provided a reliable reference signal for the ratiometric determination. Then the CLP imprinted polymeric network was formed around the B/CDs@silica by copolymerization of the APTES (functional monomer), and TEOS (crosslinking agent) in the presence of Y/CDs, CTAB (pore-generating template) and CLP (analyte template). After polymerization, CTAB and CLP were extracted by solvent from polymeric network to produce ordered porous and molecular imprinted silica shells. Specific binding sites were formed between the pores and Y/CDs were wrapped in the pores of the mesoporous silica. The resultant mMIP@YBCDs ratiometric probe displayed well-resolved dual-emission bands at 440 and 565 nm under a single wavelength excitation at 400 nm (Fig. 1 , green curve). Pure B/CDs and Y/CDs suspensions when excited at 400 nm showed single fluorescence emissions peaks centered at 440 and 565 nm respectively (Fig. 1, black and red curves respectively). Also under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation at 365 nm B/CDs and Y/CDs suspensions emit bright blue and yellow fluorescence respectively (Fig. 1 inset). The emission peaks wavelength and half-width at half-maxima of B/CDs and Y/CDs as that of related peaks of mMIP@YBCDs ratiometric probe, demonstrating that the encapsulation in silica has no obvious effect on the fluorescence properties of the CDs. Based on UV–vis absorption (Fig. S1) spectra of B/CDs and Y/CDs, 400 nm was selected as the optimal excitation wavelength.

The surface morphology, structure, and size distribution of mMIP@YBCDs were investigated by SEM (Fig. 2 a) and TEM (Fig. 2b). TEM image illustrated that mMIP@YBCDs has a highly monodispersed, spherical morphology with a diameter of about 50 nm. SEM image (Fig. 3 a inset) provided further support for the highly spherical morphology of mMIP@YBCDs.

High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of the Y/CDs (Fig. 2c) indicate spherical particles with good monodispersity and a mean diameter of ~20 nm. Also, the HR-TEM image of B/CDs (Fig. 2d) confirmed that the B/CDs are uniform nanoparticles with a size of about 2 nm. These findings are in agreement with those of previous reports (Liu et al., 2017).

The nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiment was used to confirm the mesoporous structure of mMIP@YBCDs. The resultant nitrogen isotherm (Fig. S2) displayed a typical IV type curve indicating that the pore sizes were in the mesoporous range (Orooji et al., 2017).

The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area was obtained o be 932 m2 g_1 which is considerably large (Lin et al., 2014). Due to the mesoporous nature of the imprinted layer, the mass transfer resistance is reduced and the accessibility of the recondition sites was enhanced (Kim et al., 2012).

FT-IR spectrum of mMIP@YBCDs composite is shown in Fig. S3. The strong and broad peak around 1074 cm−1 indicates the Si–O–Si asymmetric stretching. The bands at approximately 465 cm−1 were assigned to Si–O vibrations. The characteristic band at 2900 cm−1 (aliphatic C–H stretching band) and broad absorption peak at 3442 cm−1 and 1556 cm−1 (N–H band) confirm the presence of the aminopropyl group. The FT-IR (Fig. S4), SEM (Fig. 2e) and TEM (Fig. 2f) of mNIP@YBCDs are also investigated. MNIP@YBCDs and mMIP@YBCDs shared similar primary chemical compositions and polymer morphology and the observed differentiation between mMIP@YBCDs and mNIP@YBCDs is due to imprinting effect (formation of specific cavities on MIP) and not due to the observed difference in polymer morphology and compositions. These findings are inconsistent with those of previous studies (H. Liu et al., 2019b; Mehrzad-Samarin et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of mMIP@YBCDs ratiometric probe
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH (from 5.0 to 8.0) on the fluorescence intensity of MIP@YBCDs ratiometric probe was investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence peaks from both B/CDs and Y/CDs are stable in the range of pH 5.0–8.0, indicating that this probe is suitable for further usage under physiological conditions. In pH values below 5 or above 8, the fluorescence intensity ratio of the sensor was not stable. At lower pH values, the protonation of functional groups on the Y/CDs causes a decrease in fluorescence intensity (Vedamalai et al., 2014). As pH is increased above 8, the fluorescence intensity of B/CDs decreases again, probably because silica shell can be ionized at high pH values, and OH− could nucleophilically attack the surface(Amjadi et al., 2016). Then we investigated the effect of pH on CLP and mMIP@YBCDs ratiometric probe reaction. At pH 8.0, the response of mMIP@YBCDs probe to CLP (1 μg L−1) [i.e. ((F560/F440)0/(F560/F440)), where (F560/F440)0 and (F560/F440) are the fluorescence intensity ratios in the absence and presence of CLP, respectively] reached maximum (Fig. S6). Hence, phosphate buffer (pH = 8, 10 mM) was chosen as the optimal pH for the next experiments. Fig. S7 displays the correlation between the response of probe and the incubation time in the range of 0–20 min, in the presence of 1 μg L−1 of CLP. The response of mMIP@YBCDs probe increased steadily at the initial 6 min and then reached a plateau and the fluorescence intensity remained almost constant, demonstrating fast rebinding of template to recognition sites and high affinity of the imprinted layer to the template molecule. So the incubation time of 6 min was implemented in subsequent experiments. Compared to non-mesoporous silica-coated quantum dots based sensors which normally showed 15–60 min response time, this probe exhibited an extraordinarily rapid response(Amjadi et al., 2016; Sadeghi et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2017). The mesoporous nature of the imprinted silica layer can accelerate the speed of adsorption and separation of the template to specific recognition sites and rapid response was achieved. B/CDs embedded in silica shells (B/CDs@silica) are inert to CLP and only serve as reference signals for providing built-in correction to avoid environmental effects.

Under optimum condition, the response of the mMIP@YBCDs ratiometric probe toward different concentrations of CLP was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3a, the intensity of the fluorescence originated from Y/CDs (560 nm) gradually decreased with increasing CLP concentrations, while the intensity the fluorescence originating from B/CDs (440 nm) did not significantly change. Fig. 3b, shows the dependence of the fluorescence intensity ratio[((F560/F440)0/(F560/F440))] on CLP concentration. A good linear curve was obtained when CLP concentration located at the range of 0.1–3 μg L−1. As a result of fluorescence intensity ratio variation, gradual evolution of fluorescence color occurred from yellow to blue under the irradiation of UV light (Fig. 3c). The limit of detection (LOD) is defined as 0.035 μg L−1 following the 3σ IUPAC criteria. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.3% was obtained by 5 repeated analyses of 1 μg L−1solutions. To confirm our proposed method as the foremost for CLP residues sensing, the obtained results were compared with some earlier research reports. As shown in Table 1 , the analytical performances of the proposed ratiometric probe are more superior than some recently reported chromatographic and biomolecule based methods. These methods have made great progress in the detection of CLP residues; however, when faced with complex food matrices together with low concentrations of contaminants, they are limited by factors like poor specificity, slow response, high cost, and complicated sample preparation.

Calibration curve for mNIP@YBCDs response to CLP concentration was also obtained. As depicted in Fig. 3d, it is obvious that mNIP@YBCDs are also responded to CLP and linear calibration curve was achieved, but the sensitivity is much lower and the linear range is narrower (0.7–2.9 μgL−1 with a detection limit of 0.23 μg L−1). The imprinting factor (the ratio of mMIP@YBCDs and mNIP@YBCDs calibration curves slips) was calculated to be 6.05, indicating that non-specific binding of the template could not satisfy the proximity necessity for effective fluorescence decrease and the imprinting process was successful.

The superiority of the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence mMIP@YBCDs probe was proved by comparing the performance of our ratiometric probe and the single yellow-emitting mesoporous MIP. The LOD of a single yellow emitting probe was obtained to be 3.1 μgL−1. The comparison shows that the dual-emission ratiometric mMIP@YBCDs probe is more sensitive and possesses visual detection of CLP. To illustrate the efficiency of mesoporous structure, non- mesoporous structured MIP@YBCDs ratimetric probe, was prepared as control. The linear range 0.75–4 μg L−1, LOD of 0.23 μg L−1 and incubation time of 20 min were obtained for MIP@YBCDs. The sensitivity of MIP@YBCDs was significantly lower than mesoporous structured one. Also due to mesoporous structure, the mass transfer resistance is decreased greatly and the accessibility of the binding site was enhanced (Kim et al., 2012).

To evaluate the stability of the mMIP@YBCDs probe, the sensor was stored at a refrigerator (4 °C) and used for detection of CLP every other day. After 120 days, the recognition ability toward CLP remained constant without obvious change and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.9% was obtained showing that mMIP@YBCDs probe retains its activity for at least 120 days. The high stability of the ratiometric probe was attributed to the protection of imprinted silica coating as well as the self-correction effect.

Molecular orbital theory was used to elucidate the mechanism of fluorescence intensity changes due to CLP binding. In the excitation process, the electrons of Y/CDs can be excited from the ground state to the excited state. When there is no template CLP around the mMIP@YBCDs probe, the excited electrons of Y/CDs return to the valence band with generating yellow fluorescence. In the presence of the template (CLP), the amino groups (–NH2) in the molecule of APTES, in binding sites of mMIP@YBCDs, can interact with the functional groups of CLP (such as carbonyl group and nitro group) to form a Meisenheimer complex through hydrogen bonding. The interaction would lead to electron transfer between Y/CDs and CLP that greatly restrain the radiative recombination of the electron-hole pair; thus, the majority of excited electrons return to the ground state via the nonradiative decay rather than radiative decay, leading to fluorescence intensity quenching. The UV–Vis absorption band of CLP is close to the bandgap of Y/CDs (Fig. S8), so the excited electron could be directly transferred into the LUMO of the Meisenheimer complex, leading to the quenching of Y/CDs fluorescence.

The recognition ability of mMIP@YBCDs was investigated by comparing its response to CLP with that obtained for two other analog namely thiamphenicol (THM) and florfenicol (FLF) and also two other antibiotics including tetracycline (TCY) and gentamicin (GMC). The fluorescence response of mMIP@YBCDs and mNIP@YBCDs is demonstrated in Fig. 4 . For mNIP@YBCDs, the fluorescence response of probe was almost identical for all antibiotics. In the case of mMIP@YBCDs, CLP induced a prominent fluorescence ratio change. Besides THM and FLF, also caused a slight fluorescence ratio change. Whenever the addition of other antibiotics (TCY and GMC) led to almost no fluorescence ratio change. The different fluorescence response of MIP is attributed to specific recognition cavities toward CLP and its analogs, but had the highest affinity for template CLP. Therefore, the recognition sites of mMIP@YBCDs are not appropriate to accommodate other antibiotics, which results in high imprinting efficiency and intense response to CLP.

Furthermore, the anti-interference ability of this proposed ratiometric probe for detection of CLP in real food samples was investigated. The tolerable limits of interfering substances were described as a concentration that causes a relative error of less than 5%. The mMIP@YBCDs probe demonstrates high selectivity to CLP against ions and various biologically important substances (Table S1). The strong anti-interference ability indicates the capability of the proposed method for quantification of CLP in real samples.

To demonstrate the accuracy and facility of the proposed probe in real samples, the sensing of CLP in milk samples was carried out. CLP cannot be detected in milk samples. Then, the spiked experiment was carried out and the standard addition method was used to calculate the recovery of CLP. Different concentrations of CLP (3, 4, 5 μg L−1) were added into the milk samples. Then, the spiked samples were processed using our ratiometric probe. The quantitative recoveries ranged from 96.5 to 106.6%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are in the range of 3.5 to 1.1%. The results proved that this sensing method has promising potential for use in CLP detection in dairy samples.

This research introduces a new method for CLP determination that has benefits including easy to use, rapid, low cost, and high selectivity. mMIP@YBCDs probe combined the high selectivity of MIPs and the high sensitivity of ratiometric strategy and mesoporous silica and provided a highly selective and sensitive probe for CLP quantification. Variation of two fluorescence intensity ratio leads to an obvious fluorescence color change from yellow to blue, which can be used for the prompt and routine visualization of CLP. Moreover, this eco-friendly probe also exhibits high selectivity for CLP over other antibiotics. In addition, the stability, repeatability and practicability tests of the MIP-coated CDs have demonstrated good results. This developed probe was applied for the detection of CLP in real milk samples. This strategy opens up attractive perspectives for further exploration and application of the visual sensor, which, like the pH test paper, may detect antibiotic residues in milk quickly and accurately.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by a research grant (Post Doc) of the University of Tabriz (S2883-98.7.7). The authors thank the Iran University of Medical Sciences for all the support provided (Project No: (Project No: 97-4-75-13659).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
